critic_raw:
This is a carefully designed evaluation protocol. Let me work through the actual methodological issues rather than manufacturing concerns.

**Issue 1: Label maturation creates a time-varying positive prevalence that contaminates train/test comparison**

The specific claim: A 30-day maturation window produces labels that are comparably reliable across the 12-month training block and the 3-month test block.

Why this may be wrong: SOC analyst capacity, tooling, and threat intel integrations likely improve over 18 months. Early-period messages in the training block may have lower true-positive recall in their labels because older campaigns were less understood at the time they were frozen. Messages in the test block benefit from more mature threat intel, better YARA/signature coverage, and potentially retrospective enrichment from later-discovered campaign indicators. This means the negative class in training is dirtier (contains more unlabeled true positives) than the negative class in test. If so, PR-AUC on test is measured against a cleaner label surface than the model was trained on, and the reported PR-AUC overestimates operational performance while simultaneously making the training signal noisier in a way that penalizes the transformer more than the GBT — the transformer's richer representations may be partially learning from mislabeled negatives in early training months that the GBT's coarser features smooth over.

Evidence: Plot positive prevalence by month across the full 18-month window. If there is a secular upward trend in positive rate that is not explained by known campaign volume, that is evidence of improving label recall over time. Also compare how many training-window messages were retroactively re-labeled as positive after a later incident linked back to a sender — this requires checking incident ticket linkage beyond the 30-day window.

**Issue 2: Near-deduplication sensitivity analysis is proposed but not load-bearing in the design**

The specific claim: The primary evaluation (before deduplication) is not materially inflated by repeated campaign templates, and the deduplication analysis is a robustness check.

Why this may be wrong: In phishing, a single coordinated campaign can generate thousands of nearly-identical messages within a short window — same lure, same sending infrastructure, slight subject-line perturbation. If such a campaign spans the train/test boundary (e.g., starts in month 11 and continues through months 13-15), the transformer will have seen enough examples of that template to memorize it, boosting test PR-AUC substantially on what is essentially an in-distribution recall task rather than generalization to a novel threat. The GBT, relying on domain/IP reputation features, might actually generalize better to the same campaign because its features (sender reputation, URL domain) are operationally updated signals that don't require template familiarity. The deduplication analysis is mentioned as a sensitivity check but the primary reported metric is pre-dedup, which means the headline number could be driven by exactly this mechanism.

Evidence: Compute the fraction of test-set positives that share a campaign family label with any training-set positive (using the stratified campaign family breakdown already proposed). If >30% of test positives belong to campaigns already represented in training, the headline gain likely reflects memorization of seen templates rather than generalization to novel threats.

**Issue 3: Late-fusion architecture creates a silent misconfiguration risk where the transformer head degenerates and the GBT features carry the model**

The specific claim: The late-fusion result tests the hypothesis that richer sequence representations improve detection beyond handcrafted summaries.

Why this may be wrong: In a late-fusion head combining transformer embeddings with a rich numeric feature vector (URL reputation, sender reputation, attachment metadata), the fusion head can learn to down-weight the transformer's contribution if the numeric features are sufficiently predictive on the training set. The transformer encoder still gets gradient signal from the fusion loss but may contribute minimally to predictions on test. If this happens, the treatment model is not actually testing the sequence-representation hypothesis — it is testing whether adding a transformer to a GBT-equivalent feature set hurts or helps, and any gain could come entirely from the numeric features the baseline GBT also has access to, while any loss is masked by the numeric features bailing out the model.

Evidence: At evaluation time, report ablations: (a) transformer encoder + fusion head with numeric features zeroed out, (b) fusion head with transformer embedding frozen at initialization (random), (c) full model. If (b) nearly matches the full model, the transformer is not contributing. Also report the learned attention or gate weights in the fusion head if the architecture includes explicit gating.

**Issue 4: Pretrained tokenizer/vocabulary may not cover the adversarial register of phishing text, creating a systematic disadvantage for the transformer baseline that is misattributed to representational capacity**

The specific claim: A fixed pretrained tokenizer is a neutral preprocessing choice that does not systematically disadvantage the transformer.

Why this may be wrong: Phishing emails exploit homoglyphs, zero-width characters, deliberate misspellings, URL obfuscation, and HTML entity substitution specifically to defeat text-based filters. A pretrained tokenizer trained on natural-language corpora will fragment these adversarial patterns into high-entropy subword sequences that appear rare in the training vocabulary, causing the transformer to produce near-uniform embeddings for obfuscated tokens. The GBT baseline, using lexical heuristics explicitly tuned for this adversarial register (e.g., homoglyph ratios, entropy features, URL character n-grams), has an architectural advantage for exactly the cases the hypothesis is supposed to address. This means the comparison is not purely about representational power — it is also about whether the pretrained tokenizer's vocabulary coverage of adversarial text is adequate.

Evidence: Identify the subset of test positives where the email body contains known obfuscation patterns (homoglyphs, high Unicode entropy, excessive HTML entity usage). Compare PR-AUC on this subset between the transformer and GBT. If the GBT outperforms the transformer specifically on this subset while the transformer wins on clean phishing, the tokenizer coverage gap is the operative variable, not representational capacity.

**Summary of verdict reasoning:**
Issues 1 and 2 are genuine threats to validity of the primary comparison metric and cannot be resolved by design choices already described. Issue 3 is a silent misconfiguration concern that the proposed ablation structure does not fully guard against. Issue 4 is a systematic confound in the specific hypothesis being tested. All four have falsifiable empirical signatures. The methodology is thoughtful and far above average for this domain, but these issues collectively mean the reported PR-AUC comparison could favor or disfavor the transformer for reasons unrelated to the stated hypothesis.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis

### Issue 1: Label maturation creates a time-varying positive prevalence that contaminates train/test comparison

**Critic's claim:** SOC capability and threat-intel maturity improve over 18 months, making early-period negative labels dirtier (more unlabeled true positives) than late-period ones. This creates a time-varying label surface that inflates test PR-AUC and differentially penalizes transformer training relative to GBT.

**Assessment:** This is a valid and substantive concern. I cannot rebut the core mechanism. Label quality in enterprise SOCs is genuinely not stationary: YARA rules improve, threat-intel feeds expand, and retrospective campaign attribution happens. The 30-day maturation window standardizes the labeling *procedure* but does not standardize the *quality* of that procedure across time. Earlier windows will have lower true-positive recall, and the direction of bias is exactly as described — the test-set negative class is cleaner, inflating reported PR-AUC. The differential effect on the transformer is also plausible: noisier negatives in training inject more contradictory gradient signal into a model with larger capacity and more degrees of freedom, whereas GBT's coarser features may be less sensitive to individual mislabeled points.

**Counter-argument and partial mitigation:** The proposed stratification by month and the sensitivity analysis around campaign families partially address this, but the design does not include an explicit label-quality audit. The critic's proposed diagnostic is correct: plot positive prevalence by month and check for secular upward trends not explained by known campaign volume. Additionally, if the organization's ticketing system permits, tracking messages retroactively re-labeled after incident linkage beyond the 30-day window is the right control. These diagnostics are not currently specified as required reporting in the methodology — they are left implicit.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** Whether the magnitude of this effect is large enough to flip the direction of the PR-AUC comparison, or whether it merely narrows the gap. This requires the empirical label-quality audit to bound.

**Concession level:** High. This is a real threat to validity that the current methodology does not structurally control for.

---

### Issue 2: Near-deduplication sensitivity analysis is proposed but not load-bearing in the design

**Critic's claim:** A campaign spanning the train/test boundary allows the transformer to memorize templates, boosting test PR-AUC on in-distribution recall rather than genuine generalization. The dedup check is a secondary analysis; the headline metric is pre-dedup.

**Assessment:** Also valid. The methodology correctly anticipates this concern by specifying the deduplication sensitivity analysis, but the critic is right that the architectural position of that check — as a robustness addendum rather than the primary reported metric — means the headline number is vulnerable to exactly this inflation mechanism. The transformer's sequence-level representations are precisely the features that would memorize a repeated lure template, whereas the GBT's reputation-based features would generalize differently (and possibly better, as the critic notes, because operational reputation signals are updated independently of template familiarity).

**Counter-argument:** The methodology does specify stratified analysis by campaign family, which at minimum allows the reader to see how much of the gain is concentrated in campaigns present in both training and test. The campaign-family breakdown is already a required output, not optional. However, the critic's specific threshold test is useful: if more than ~30% of test positives share a campaign family with training positives, the primary PR-AUC number requires the dedup result as a co-primary metric, not a footnote.

**Partial defense:** In enterprise phishing, not all cross-boundary campaigns are 'template memorization' cases — some involve genuine infrastructure and sender overlap that a deployed model *should* generalize from. Completely deduplicating all campaign-family overlap would overcorrect. The right framing is: report both pre-dedup and post-dedup PR-AUC as co-primary metrics with explicit disclosure of the campaign-overlap fraction, and let the reader interpret the gap between them as the memorization-driven component.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** The actual fraction of cross-boundary campaign overlap in this specific 18-month window. That is an empirical question about this organization's threat landscape.

**Concession level:** Moderate-to-high. The design intent was to include dedup as a robustness check, but the critic is correct that it should be elevated to co-primary status given the plausible magnitude of the inflation.

---

### Issue 3: Late-fusion architecture creates a silent misconfiguration risk where the transformer head degenerates and the GBT features carry the model

**Critic's claim:** In the late-fusion architecture, the fusion head may learn to down-weight transformer embeddings if numeric features are sufficiently predictive, meaning a positive treatment result does not confirm the sequence-representation hypothesis.

**Assessment:** This is a well-known failure mode of late-fusion architectures and the critic is correct to flag it. The methodology states the hypothesis clearly — that richer sequence representations improve detection *beyond* handcrafted summaries — but does not specify ablations to verify the transformer component is actually contributing. Without the ablations the critic describes, a positive PR-AUC result for the treatment model is consistent with both (a) the sequence representations adding genuine signal and (b) the numeric features dominating while the transformer is a dead weight or minor contributor.

**Counter-argument:** This is not a flaw in the evaluation protocol per se — it is a gap in the ablation specification. The fix is straightforward and the critic has already specified it correctly: (a) transformer encoder with numeric features zeroed, (b) fusion head with frozen random transformer embeddings, (c) full model. If (b) nearly matches the full model, the transformer is not contributing. This ablation suite should be part of the required analysis, not left implicit. The methodology's stated purpose is to test the hypothesis about representational power, so these ablations are necessary, not optional.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** Whether the numeric features in this specific feature stack are rich enough to dominate in the fusion head. In mail-gateway settings with strong sender/URL reputation features, this is plausible.

**Concession level:** Moderate. The concern is real and the ablation gap is a genuine omission, but it is correctable by adding required ablations to the specification without redesigning the experiment. I concede that as written, the methodology does not structurally guard against this.

---

### Issue 4: Pretrained tokenizer/vocabulary may not cover adversarially obfuscated phishing text, giving the GBT a systematic advantage on the cases where the hypothesis is most salient

**Critic's claim:** Homoglyphs, zero-width characters, HTML entity substitution, and URL obfuscation produce high-entropy subword fragmentation in a natural-language-pretrained tokenizer, causing near-uniform transformer embeddings for exactly the obfuscated tokens the GBT's lexical heuristics are designed to detect.

**Assessment:** This is a legitimate concern about the scope of the hypothesis test. The methodology uses 'safe text extracts' which partially mitigates the HTML entity and rendering-layer obfuscation concern (if safe extraction is done correctly, some of these are normalized before tokenization). However, homoglyphs and high-Unicode-entropy strings will still pass through and fragment badly in standard tokenizers. The critic is right that this creates an asymmetric disadvantage for the transformer on a specific and important subset of test positives — specifically, the sophisticated evasion attempts that a stronger sequence model *should* handle better if the tokenizer coverage were adequate.

**Counter-argument:** The 'safe text extracts' pipeline should include Unicode normalization (NFC/NFKC) and homoglyph normalization as preprocessing steps — if those are applied, the tokenizer disadvantage is substantially reduced. Whether the methodology specifies this is not entirely clear; the description says 'safe text extracts' but does not enumerate the normalization steps. If Unicode and homoglyph normalization are included in the safe-extraction pipeline, the tokenizer gap narrows considerably. However, the methodology does not explicitly state this, so the critic's concern stands as written.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** The fraction of test positives relying on obfuscation techniques versus clean social-engineering lures (which are increasingly the dominant phishing modality and would favor the transformer). If the test set is mostly clean lures with sophisticated social engineering, the tokenizer gap may be small in practice. If the test set has a substantial evasion-technique component, the gap is operationally significant.

**Concession level:** Moderate. The concern is real but partially dependent on what normalization is included in the safe-extraction pipeline (which should be specified more precisely) and on the empirical composition of the test set. The critic's proposed subset analysis is the right diagnostic.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Summarizing the concession structure:
- Issue 1: High concession. Time-varying label quality is a real, uncontrolled threat to the primary comparison metric. The methodology lacks a required label-quality audit.
- Issue 2: Moderate-to-high concession. Campaign-boundary overlap is real; the deduplication check should be co-primary, not secondary.
- Issue 3: Moderate concession. The ablation gap is real and correctible, but requires adding required ablations to the specification.
- Issue 4: Moderate concession. Tokenizer coverage is a real concern whose magnitude depends on preprocessing details and test-set composition.

Issues 1 and 2 are threats to the *validity of the primary metric* (PR-AUC on the test period) and cannot be resolved by argument — they require empirical audits of label quality over time and campaign-overlap quantification. Issues 3 and 4 require empirical ablations and subset analyses to bound. None of these invalidate the experimental design in principle, but all four produce falsifiable empirical signatures whose magnitudes are unknown without running the experiment. The methodology is architecturally sound but incompletely specified at the level of required diagnostics, and the primary reported metric is potentially inflated by mechanisms the current spec does not structurally control.

This is not a case where the methodology is fatally flawed — the right response is to extend the required analysis suite (label audit, co-primary dedup metric, ablation suite, obfuscation-subset analysis). But whether the transformer actually outperforms GBT on a hypothesis-clean basis cannot be determined from the current specification alone. The verdict is `empirical_test_agreed`.
all_issues_raised: Label maturation quality likely improves over the 18-month window, making negative-class labels noisier in early training than in test and creating a time-varying label surface that inflates test PR-AUC and differentially degrades transformer training signal.

Campaign-spanning train/test boundary allows the transformer to memorize seen templates and report inflated PR-AUC on nominally future data; the deduplication sensitivity check is not the primary reported metric so this inflation is not structurally controlled.

Late-fusion architecture can silently degenerate so that numeric features dominate predictions and the transformer contributes minimally, meaning a positive result does not confirm the sequence-representation hypothesis.

Fixed pretrained tokenizer may have poor vocabulary coverage of adversarially obfuscated phishing text, giving the GBT baseline a systematic advantage on exactly the cases where richer sequence representations should matter most.
all_issues_adjudicated: Label maturation quality likely improves over 18 months, making early-period negative labels noisier than test-period labels in a way the 30-day freeze procedure does not control, creating a time-varying label surface that inflates test PR-AUC and differentially degrades transformer training signal — the defender conceded this at high confidence and the methodology lacks a required label-quality audit to bound the effect.

Campaign-spanning train/test boundary allows the transformer to memorize seen templates and report inflated PR-AUC on nominally future data; the deduplication sensitivity analysis is structurally positioned as a secondary check rather than co-primary metric, leaving the headline number uncontrolled against this inflation mechanism — the defender conceded this at moderate-to-high confidence.

Late-fusion architecture can silently degenerate so that numeric features dominate predictions and the transformer contributes minimally, and the methodology does not specify required ablations to verify the transformer's contribution — the defender conceded this as a genuine omission correctable by adding ablations but not corrected as written.

Fixed pretrained tokenizer may have poor vocabulary coverage of adversarially obfuscated phishing text, giving the GBT a systematic advantage on exactly the evasion-heavy subset where richer sequence representations should matter most — the defender acknowledged the concern is real and partially dependent on unspecified normalization steps and test-set composition.
